text: Senegal's Annual Agricultural Survey is a national official survey covering all 45 departments. It includes rainfed agriculture, horticulture, and fruit-growing households, so it is a reliable backbone source for describing official agricultural production context.

The Center and Niayes zones are the main source of Senegal's fresh mango exports. The value-chain study describes these zones as the leading export-oriented production area and links them to the July to September marketing window for the EU fresh market.

Casamance produces a large share of Senegal's mango volume, but much of that fruit is not exported. The study links these losses and unsold volumes to fruit fly pressure, other pests and diseases, overripeness, fragmented collection, and weak post-harvest logistics.

The Senegal mango value-chain study identifies Casamance as a strong candidate for processing rather than only fresh export. It explicitly highlights juice, dried fruit, and frozen cubes as realistic processing directions if logistics and farmer organization improve.

ANACIM bulletins show a strong rainfall gradient across Senegal, with much drier northern stations and much wetter southern stations. That regional climate difference should shape how the assistant reasons about drying risk, pest pressure, and crop handling conditions.

The late-August 2023 ANACIM bulletin mentions pest pressure on millet in Kaffrine. This type of bulletin is useful operational context for the assistant because it ties climate monitoring to actual field alerts.

The IITA Senegal status report describes a high aflatoxin risk pattern when wet-harvested groundnuts remain on the ground for several days before proper drying and storage. This is strong RAG context for explaining why drying discipline matters in the groundnut chain.

Aflasafe SN01 was tested in Diourbel and Kaolack and the report says it reduced aflatoxin contamination by more than 80 percent, with even greater reduction during storage. This should be treated as technical mitigation context, not as a direct model label.

FAO references Senegal millet storage performance in traditional granaries and cites relatively low storage-loss figures for millet compared with sorghum in the same context. This is useful background when the assistant explains cereal storage behavior and anomaly thresholds.

FAO cites Senegal studies where properly dried and threshed millet and sorghum mixed with sand had lower storage losses. This is the kind of practical low-cost storage context that belongs in the RAG knowledge base.

Recent Senegal government communication on the groundnut chain shows active investment in collection and cleaning equipment. That kind of policy and operational modernization context is valuable for the assistant when it explains current sector priorities.

FAO's mango post-harvest guide emphasizes careful harvesting, avoiding bruising, removing damaged fruit during sorting, and using temperature-controlled storage. This is suitable as general best-practice context for RAG, but not as Senegal-specific ground truth.
